title: Recurrence without enactment
subtitle: Eighty-nine years of the public-library obligation
source: ILA proceedings; the CSL Compendium; the Rajya Sabha record. RTR-WP-008.
caption: Each marker is a documented event, 1936–2025, placed on the tier of the State's answer: a demand at the top, then, further down, a deferral (shelved), a refusal (declined), or an inversion (reversed — the 1993 fee). The dashed rule is the outcome the whole sequence sought — a binding central library Act — and no marker ever lands on it; the further a marker sits below it, the deeper the betrayal. Events are evenly spaced so the sequence stays legible; the year on each point is the true date.

data.t: Bombay Public Libraries Bill No. XXIV introduced — and not further considered.
Library Development Committee, Bombay: a library cess weighed and declined; only permissive legislation proposed.
ILA, 6th conference: the Act must be central, mandatory, tax-funded and free. “The Government of the country must finance the scheme.”
Sinha Advisory Committee: free access “the right of every citizen … as his fundamental right,” funded by a cess — then deferred “gradually within a period of twenty years.”
Fourth Plan Working Group: Rs 30.99 crore, a Directorate of Libraries in every State, and a Draft Bill prepared for consideration.
National Policy on Library & Information Systems drafted — and never laid before Parliament.
Obligation inverted: libraries “may charge Re. 1 to Rs. 10 as nominal membership fee.” Declared a “State subject.”
The profession asks whether it must “wait till someone files a PIL in the Supreme Court.”
Rajya Sabha: “The subject ‘Libraries’ comes under State list. No such data is maintained. … There is no such plan.”
